nativeRange.habitat: Coral reefs; typically found among rubble and reef substrate in shallow coastal waters

diet.notes: Has been documented eating small crustaceans including amphipods, copepods, mysis shrimp, and brine shrimp in captivity. Benefits considerably from an established tank with live rock that supports a natural population of microcrustaceans. Tends to accept frozen or freeze-dried meaty foods such as mysis and brine shrimp, and small carnivore pellets. Generally advised to feed small amounts multiple times per day, especially during the initial acclimation period.

summary: The Gold Neon Eviota Goby is a tiny Indo-Pacific reef fish, generally considered an excellent choice for nano reef aquariums. Its opaque body is marked with red vertical bands and striking gold-rimmed eyes.
careNotes:
The Gold Neon Eviota Goby is one of the smallest gobies available in the hobby, generally reaching no more than about 2.5 cm. It is generally considered reef-safe and tends to be entirely peaceful toward corals and most invertebrates. Best kept singly, as a pair, or in a small group introduced simultaneously to reduce conspecific squabbles; individuals have been known to be mildly territorial toward conspecifics when space is limited.

This species tends to do best in a well-established reef aquarium with abundant live rock and a healthy population of microcrustaceans (copepods, amphipods). Tank-raised specimens are occasionally available and tend to acclimate more readily. Due to its very small size, it may be vulnerable to strong filter intakes or overflows; sponge pre-filters are advisable.

Lifespan is reported to be notably short for a captive fish — anywhere from a few months up to roughly two years, consistent with the characteristically brief lifespans documented in the Eviota genus. Aquarists should be prepared for a relatively short-lived fish and not mistake natural senescence for disease.

Gobies of this genus are confirmed escape risks and require a tightly-fitting lid or mesh cover — small bodies and jumping behavior are well documented across the genus.

additional.name: FishBase / WoRMS
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy: Order Gobiiformes, Family Gobiidae. Maximum recorded total length 21 mm (2.1 cm), consistent with hobby sources. Confirmed native range spans Indo-Pacific including Mariana Islands, Marshall Islands, Ryukyu Islands, Philippines, and Australia.
